g.data-narration: Welcome to Deadlock Avoidance. We will explore safe states, Resource Allocation Graphs, and Banker's Algorithm.
Deadlock avoidance dynamically examines resource allocation state before granting requests, ensuring system stays safe.
g.text: Unlike prevention (static rules), avoidance allows all four conditions but makes
smart dynamic decisions at runtime to stay in a safe state.
Key: Each process declares maximum resources needed in advance.

g.data-narration: A safe state guarantees no deadlock - a sequence exists where all processes can complete. Unsafe may lead to deadlock.
The relationship shows deadlock is always within unsafe states, while unsafe states are within all system states.
For single-instance resources, we use a modified Resource Allocation Graph with three types of edges.
The RAG algorithm converts claim edges to request edges, then checks for cycles before granting allocation.
When a cycle exists in the RAG, granting the request would lead to unsafe state. No cycle means safe allocation.
For multiple resource instances, Banker's Algorithm simulates allocation before committing, ensuring safety.
g.text: Named after a banker who never lends more than can be safely recovered.
Proposed by Edsger Dijkstra - ensures system never enters unsafe state.

g.data-narration: Four data structures drive Banker's Algorithm: Available, Max, Allocation, and Need matrices.
Need matrix equals Maximum demand minus current Allocation. This tells how much more each process can request.
Safety Algorithm finds a safe sequence by simulating resource allocation with Work and Finish arrays.
If all processes can finish (all Finish[i]=true), system is safe. Otherwise, unsafe state leads to potential deadlock.
Let's work through Banker's Algorithm with 5 processes and 3 resource types: A(10), B(5), C(7).
g.text: Worked Example: System State at T0

g.data-narration: Available = Total - Allocated = (3,3,2). P1 can run first because Need(1,2,2) ≤ Work(3,3,2).
Complete safe sequence: P1→P3→P4→P2→P0. After each process finishes, more resources become available.
When a process requests resources, four checks occur: Need, Available, tentative allocation, then safety test.
Key takeaways: Avoidance uses dynamic checks unlike prevention's static rules. Banker's ensures safe states.
